questions.question: What is the primary architectural purpose of a `Dockerfile` objectively?
options: It establishes a native WebSocket connection securely between microservices

It acts as a literal mathematical recipe defining exactly how to package your application code, alongside a micro-Operating System and strict system dependencies natively, into a standardized, immutable 'Image' file locally
It compiles Markdown into React natively
It permanently enforces linting boundaries exclusively
questions.answer: It acts as a literal mathematical recipe defining exactly how to package your application code, alongside a micro-Operating System and strict system dependencies natively, into a standardized, immutable 'Image' file locally
questions.explanation: Instead of writing a 10-page Word Document instructing a junior developer how to install Node 18, set environment variables, and run `npm start`, the Dockerfile automates that entire process cryptographically.

questions.question: In a Node.js Dockerfile natively, why is it considered the absolute industry standard mathematically to copy `package.json` and execute `npm install` BEFORE explicitly copying the rest of the application source code?
options: Because NPM mathematically requires strict network prioritization locally

To explicitly leverage Docker's native Layer Caching mechanism. Since the package.json file rarely mathematically changes, Docker automatically caches the massive `node_modules` installation layer entirely, skipping it and saving 5 minutes every time you casually re-build the image after modifying a JavaScript file
Because `npm install` inherently deletes source code securely
Because Docker inherently executes the file randomly otherwise
questions.answer: To explicitly leverage Docker's native Layer Caching mechanism. Since the package.json file rarely mathematically changes, Docker automatically caches the massive `node_modules` installation layer entirely, skipping it and saving 5 minutes every time you casually re-build the image after modifying a JavaScript file
questions.explanation: If you do `COPY . .` first, Docker detects that `server.js` changed, mathematically invalidating that entire layer and everything below it, forcing it to violently re-download all 500MB of `node_modules` literally every single build.

questions.question: When defining the base operating system image specifically (e.g., `FROM node:18`), why is appending `-alpine` (e.g., `FROM node:18-alpine`) almost universally recommended natively?
options: Alpine fundamentally overrides global JavaScript errors dynamically
Alpine inherently installs Python 3 natively

Alpine explicitly utilizes a highly stripped-down minimalist Linux distribution locally that is often under 5MB radically natively, massively reducing network transfer durations, storage costs, and severe security vulnerabilities dynamically
Alpine enables TypeScript explicitly
questions.answer: Alpine explicitly utilizes a highly stripped-down minimalist Linux distribution locally that is often under 5MB radically natively, massively reducing network transfer durations, storage costs, and severe security vulnerabilities dynamically
questions.explanation: A standard Ubuntu-based `node:18` image contains bash processors, graphical utilities, and text editors amounting to 1 Gigabyte natively. If a hacker exploits your app, having those tools available is deadly. Alpine ships entirely without them.

questions.question: What explicitly is a 'Multi-Stage Build' locally in the context of creating a Dockerfile specifically for a React frontend natively?
options: A script that runs Docker inside of a virtual machine directly

A structural technique where an initial 'Builder' image installs massive compiling dependencies securely, executes `npm run build`, and then entirely discards that heavy environment directly, literally copying exactly only the final static `.js` files natively into a radically tiny production web server image
A method mathematically ensuring Redis caches strictly
A system specifically orchestrating Git branches locally
questions.answer: A structural technique where an initial 'Builder' image installs massive compiling dependencies securely, executes `npm run build`, and then entirely discards that heavy environment directly, literally copying exactly only the final static `.js` files natively into a radically tiny production web server image
questions.explanation: A React development environment contains Webpack and Babel taking up 800MB. But production React just needs an Nginx server streaming a 2MB index.html file. Multi-stage physically discards the 800MB bloat perfectly.

questions.question: What is precisely the architectural difference mathematically between a Docker 'Image' and a Docker 'Container' globally?
options: An image mathematically rests in AWS primarily, a container is strictly local exclusively
An Image natively contains exclusively database logic directly

An Image is explicitly the static, completely immutable, packaged artifact file template strictly resting on your hard drive. A Container explicitly is the mathematically live, running process explicitly executing that template completely entirely isolated from your host OS natively.
An Image specifically compiles Markdown dynamically natively
questions.answer: An Image is explicitly the static, completely immutable, packaged artifact file template strictly resting on your hard drive. A Container explicitly is the mathematically live, running process explicitly executing that template completely entirely isolated from your host OS natively.
questions.explanation: Think of an Image as the generic compiled `.exe` or `.app` physical file uniquely resting on your disk. Think of the Container mathematically as the actual live application running perfectly inside your Task Manager taking up RAM.
